channel.title: Field Notes on Spyke Space
channel.description: Recent content in Field Notes on Spyke Space

item.title: The Language of Music
item.description: When I left streaming, I thought I was giving something up. For years, music arrived effortlessly. Every Friday there were new releases. Recommendations quietly appeared. Artists I had never heard of somehow found their way into my headphones. It was easy to mistake convenience for discovery. Without the algorithm, the silence felt strange. How would I know what was new? I started browsing instead. Record labels. Music magazines. Discogs. Not because I was looking for something specific. Simply because I wanted to remain in conversation with music. The experience was messier than I expected. Most recommendations weren’t for me. Some albums lasted thirty seconds. Others I didn’t understand at all. Every now and then, though, I’d stop. Wait… who is this? That moment felt different. Not because I had found my next favorite artist. Because I hadn’t known I was looking. I realized I wasn’t trying to recreate the algorithm. I was trying to recover wandering. Then something unexpected happened. Burning CDs stopped being a nostalgic project. Buying a record stopped being about collecting. I found myself thinking less about ownership and more about what these objects made possible. A friend and I decided to build a shared record library. Not my records. Not his records. Ours. A collection that could move between two homes, gathering stories along the way. One person discovers an album. The other says, “You have to hear this.” A record leaves the shelf. A table becomes a listening room. For an hour, music isn’t background. It’s the reason two people are in the same place. I used to think a library was a building full of books. Working in libraries taught me otherwise. A library is an agreement. No one person has to own everything for everyone to have access. Perhaps a shared record library is the same kind of agreement. Not about possession. About participation. I’ve started wondering if every passion has a language. Photography. Gardening. Libraries. Music. Learning the language isn’t just learning the vocabulary. It’s finding people who speak it too. Maybe that’s what I’ve been searching for all along. Not a better way to listen. A way to make room for relationships around a shared love of listening. The records matter. The CDs matter. The equipment matters. But only because they give the conversation somewhere to begin.

item.title: Making Room
item.description:
The first contribution to the new Seed Library wasn’t a seed.

It was a photograph.

A local gardener brought in a picture of their garden with a short note encouraging others to try xeriscaping. We pinned it to a small community board above the seed cabinet. It became the first story in what I hope will become a growing collection.

A few days earlier, I had been removing things.

The checkout log disappeared. We had never really used the information it collected. The brochure became a bookmark. I stopped worrying about maintaining a detailed seed inventory. One by one, I asked whether each part of the Seed Library existed to serve the library or to serve the person standing in front of it.

Some things stayed.

The bookmark stayed because someone might tuck it inside a gardening book and discover it again next spring. A featured gardening book remained because this is still a library. A small cork board stayed because it offered an invitation instead of another instruction.

Everything else quietly fell away.

I had been thinking about stewardship as preserving things.

Preserving the forms.

Preserving the procedures.

Preserving the pieces we’d always had.

But stewardship isn’t only about keeping.

Sometimes it’s about making room.

Making room for curiosity.

Making room for participation.

Making room for people to surprise you.

The first photograph convinced me that people don’t always want another place to record what they’ve taken.

Sometimes they’re looking for a place to leave behind what they’ve learned.

The old Seed Library asked patrons to document a transaction.

The new Seed Library invited them to contribute to a community.

Those sound similar, but they create entirely different experiences.

I don’t know how full that little cork board will become over the years.

I do know that the first thing pinned to it wasn’t a reminder of what left the library.

It was evidence of what came back.

item.title: Outreach Starts Inside
item.description:
I sat down to write a conference proposal with two days left before the deadline.

I wasn’t starting from nothing. In fact, I had almost the opposite problem. I had too much. Two years of projects, experiments, frustrations, maps, partnerships, bookmobile stops, community events, program kits, websites, checklists, conversations, and observations all wanted to be included. Somewhere in all of it was the story I wanted to tell. I just couldn’t see its shape.

So I started talking.

Not about the proposal, but about the work.

I remembered the uncertainty of stepping into a brand-new outreach librarian role while the library itself was still evolving. I remembered believing that my work could only ever be as good as the tools I had to work with. I remembered helping improve collections, signage, spaces, and workflows because I couldn’t imagine inviting someone to discover a library that wasn’t yet ready to tell its own story.

Then another memory surfaced.

There is a particular feeling that has followed me through outreach work. It doesn’t happen when the weather changes or a visit moves to another parking lot. Those moments are expected. This feeling arrives when I find myself asking, What am I actually doing here?

Not because something has gone wrong.

Because I know the library has something meaningful for the people standing in front of me, and yet I don’t have it with me.

That question became the thread that connected everything else.

The grab-and-go program kits. The outdoor reading room. The mobile makerspace ideas. The website. The intake forms. Even the language we use to describe our services. None of those projects existed in isolation. They were all attempts to close the distance between what the library is capable of and what outreach is able to share.

Somewhere in that conversation another sentence appeared, almost by accident.

Outreach is looking inside the library.

It surprised me because it sounded backwards.

We often imagine outreach as the work that happens outside our walls. But before we can carry the library into the community, we have to understand what we’re carrying. Outreach isn’t separate from the library. It’s a reflection of it.

The more I thought about it, the more I realized that I hadn’t been building a bookmobile service. I had been building a mobile expression of the library itself.

Books will always be part of that. But so are storytimes, makerspace activities, reading spaces, conversations, discovery, creativity, and belonging. My outreach work became less about asking, What should I take with me? and more about asking, How can the library show up here?

By the end of the afternoon, the conference proposal had almost written itself.

Not because I finally found the right words.

Because I finally understood what I’d been building all along.

The proposal was simply the first time I said it out loud.

item.title: Water Bubbles
item.description:
For months I thought I was trying to make bubble bath.

The experiments looked reasonable enough. Different ingredients. Different ratios. A notebook full of observations. Progress measured one batch at a time.

The funny part is that I wasn’t really studying bath foam.

I was studying bubbles in the air.

I already knew how soap bubbles behaved. I knew about bubble wands and giant bubbles, about blowing gently for larger bubbles and harder for smaller ones. Without realizing it, I carried those assumptions into the bathtub.

Bath foam refused to cooperate.

Eventually I stopped experimenting. The notebook went back into the closet. I bought a bottle of commercial bubble bath because it was easy. I played with catnip bubbles with Hino. I joined toddlers at the library, popping bubbles in the air. I wasn’t trying to solve anything anymore.

Then I came back.

The realization was almost embarrassingly simple.

These aren’t air bubbles. They’re water bubbles.

That sentence changed everything.

I stopped asking how to trap air.

I started asking how to build foam.

The answer turned out to be wonderfully uncomplicated: two parts decyl glucoside and one part cocamidopropyl betaine.

But the recipe wasn’t really the discovery.

The discovery was that understanding often arrives when we stop forcing one kind of knowledge to explain another.

Sometimes two things share a name but not a nature.

Air bubbles and bath foam.

Libraries and bookstores.

Questions and answers.

The world keeps reminding me that every subject deserves to be encountered on its own terms.

The bubbles were beautiful.

But what they really taught me was how to begin again.

item.title: Tending Subjects
item.description: I’ve been thinking a lot about how to organize Field Notes. At first, I assumed I needed to catalog the topics I wrote about. A note about bubbles should probably be tagged with bubbles. A note about fragrance should probably mention perfume. Before long I found myself imagining endless lists of increasingly specific things to maintain. It felt strangely familiar. Libraries have spent generations thinking about how people discover information. One of the first lessons they teach is that not everything deserves the same kind of stewardship. A search engine is remarkably good at finding words like bubbles, rainstorms, OceanLab, or linen. Those things already live naturally in the writing. Every sentence quietly becomes an index. What deserves more care are the subjects. A bubble experiment might belong to Physical Science. A reflection on organizing this website might belong to Library Science. Those subjects change slowly. They become places where seemingly unrelated Field Notes can discover one another over time. I realized I wasn’t trying to catalog everything. I was tending a landscape. The details are free to grow wild. The landscapes are cared for. There’s something comforting about that. It feels less like organizing a website and more like caring for a garden. Search can wander through the flowers. I only have to tend the paths between them.

item.title: The Library
item.description:
Libraries have become a huge part of my life. They always have been.

People sometimes ask how I ended up becoming a librarian. Colleagues ask. Patrons ask. Friends ask.

I always tell them the same story.

When I was a kid, my best friends were my sister and Mouse. We’d disappear into the stacks together and do exactly the things you weren’t supposed to do. We’d sneak snacks, settle into a quiet corner, and disappear into stories.

I’ve always thought that was the perfect use of a library.

Libraries are strange places. They have a way of dissolving time and space until all that’s left is imagination, observation, and reflection—three things I seem to organize much of my life around.

Working in libraries hasn’t changed that belief. If anything, it’s strengthened it.

Part of the reason I share so openly is because I don’t think of myself as library police. I don’t create rules for how people should engage with imagination. I don’t decide what someone should wonder about.

I’m simply a host.

The library belongs to the people who walk through its doors. My job is to help them find what they’re looking for—or sometimes, the thing they didn’t know they needed until they stumbled across it.

Sometimes that thing is a book. Sometimes it’s a quiet place to think. Sometimes it’s just a place to sneak a snack.

My personal library is a little different.

Its shelves are filled according to curiosity rather than any system. Books. Comics. Old magazines. Things that might make your grandma clutch her rosary. Folklore. Anthropology. Architecture. Fantasy. Strange ideas. Beautiful ideas. Contradictory ideas.

I collect the things that make me stop and wonder about the world.

Someone once said that books let you live hundreds of lives.

I think that’s true.

Maybe that’s why I keep collecting them.

If you’ve wandered this far and something on my shelves catches your curiosity, you’re welcome to ask about a library card at the Front Desk. I’d be happy to share a few of the quieter shelves.

item.title: The Frame
item.description:
Aren’t We Lucky?

We spend our days sending each other little pieces of life.

A goofy selfie that made me miss your face. A margarita glowing against a San Antonio sunset that somehow turns into plans for SeaWorld. A picture of Figs and Loki doing something ridiculous. A random chicken sandwich that looked particularly excellent.

The conversation keeps moving, and those little moments are carried away by the current. Knowing myself, I probably won’t ever scroll back far enough to see them again.

So I bought a shared digital picture frame.

It sits just inside the threshold of my home, where I pass by it several times a day. Every time I walk past, it’s quietly showing me little glimpses into the lives of the people I care about.

I love it.

I can’t tell you how happy it makes me to see Figs and Loki, Jayden, Gary, Kobe, Theodore, and all the little moments that have already started collecting there. It still amazes me that someone can notice something, share it, and a few moments later it quietly becomes part of my day.

The best part is that the photos don’t have to be extraordinary.

In fact, I hope they aren’t.

Show me your day. Your pets. Your shopping trip. The hot dude or dudette you happened to notice while you were out. A rainy morning. A project you’re proud of. The weird thing you found at the thrift store. The random chicken sandwich that looked too good not to photograph. Something you took a picture of without really knowing why. Those are my favorite kinds of photos.

They’re traces.

One observer quietly saying to another,

“This made me stop.”

I’d love to see it too.

The Frame isn’t an archive.

I already have journals, photographs, and stories that preserve history. The Frame serves a different purpose. It lets the people I care about quietly continue showing up in my day. A little window into whatever they’ve been noticing lately.

Every photo reminds me that, for just a moment, you thought,

“Spyke might appreciate this.”

That simple thought carries more meaning than the photograph itself.

Because if it mattered enough to make you stop… …I’d love to stop there with you.

If you’re part of my life and would like to leave the occasional trace on the Frame, don’t be surprised when I send you an invitation.

I just really miss your face.

item.title: See_You_At_Sunrise
item.description:
Day 1

Inside out. Did major cutting on my shirt. Perfect! Perfect! Forgot what we ate day one. Oh! I think it was peanut butter oats with apple crisp topping at Winnie and Ethels Diner.

Day 2

Squirtle squad! Blowing bubble attacks on people was fun! Another sunrise and another lineup schedule that was awesome. I know I had something but I don’t remember what. Oh! A donut, fruity pebbles. It was loaded with frosting and rolled in cereal. I had a vanilla coffee with almond milk I think. We hunted collectibles, visiting all the stages. The car ride home was looooong.

Day 3

Freezing wind! I survived. Made it to the car. Lumpia and bulgogi.

item.title: Terminal Ritual
item.description: Small remote magic tonight. Is this digital scrying? Anyway here’s a photo of the sun from my window.

item.title: Sunday Morning
item.description:
Hydra engaged. Gave it a snack and then it wouldn’t stop following me. This is a terribly coded attempt at metaphor for I logged into Disney+ at home (subscription accidentally renewed, not mine, but might as well use it). Well… when in rome… I searched for Disney Classics and got Scrubs, Designing Women, Bewitched (a good one). Guess I’ll be keeping an eye out for DVDs. I just can’t with 20 tags for your whole library. Anyway where is filtering? Disney’s Junk Drawer.

Anyway, the demon and I posted a reel. But he’s bound to the Darkroom because he gets into everything and I’m bound to him. We’ll make the best of it.

item.title: First Sighting
item.description: Perched at the dais the beams find me. Dust motes come to my rescue flying between me and discovery. I’ve just got one more thing to do here.
